mission.title: Install and Configure Rook on Kubernetes
mission.description: Rook is a cloud-native storage orchestrator for Kubernetes that automates the deployment and management of Ceph storage. Installing Rook allows you to leverage Ceph's capabilities for file, block, and object storage in your Kubernetes clusters.

steps.title: Add the Rook Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Rook Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add rook-release https://charts.rook.io/release
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Rook using Helm
steps.description:
Install Rook in the 'rook' namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm install rook-release/rook --namespace rook --create-namespace --version v1.19.2
```

steps.title: Verify the Rook installation
steps.description:
Check that the Rook operator pod is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n rook
```

steps.title: Check the Rook operator logs
steps.description:
To ensure that Rook is functioning correctly, check the logs of the Rook operator:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app=rook-operator -n rook
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Rook will show the Rook operator pod running in the 'rook' namespace. You should also see the resource limits applied to the operator deployment and the logs indicating that Rook is operating correctly.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add rook-release https://charts.rook.io/release
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Rook Helm release from the 'rook' namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall rook -n rook
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) associated with Rook and any persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep rook.io | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all -n rook
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'rook' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace rook
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of your Rook installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n rook -o yaml > rook-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Rook to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade rook-release/rook --namespace rook --version v1.19.3
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Rook operator pod is running after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n rook
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Rook operator pod is in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app=rook-operator -n rook
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate configuration issues or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Rook operator not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Rook operator pod is not starting, ensure that the necessary permissions are set. Check the events for the pod:
```bash
kubectl describe pod -l app=rook-operator -n rook
``` 
Look for any permission denied errors or missing resources.

troubleshooting.title: StorageClass not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues with the StorageClass, verify that it exists and is correctly configured:
```bash
kubectl get storageclass
``` 
Ensure that the StorageClass used by Rook is present and correctly defined.

troubleshooting.title: Ceph cluster not healthy
troubleshooting.description:
If the Ceph cluster is reported as unhealthy, check the status of the Ceph cluster:
```bash
kubectl -n rook exec -it deploy/rook-ceph-tools -- ceph status
``` 
Investigate any warnings or errors reported in the Ceph status output.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed.
